Problem:
\problemname{A-Mazing Puzzle}

The Severely Challenging Archaeological Exploration Division (SCArED) specializes in the investigation of archaeological sites that are too difficult, dangerous or downright terrifying for humans to examine firsthand.  Instead they send in one or more mobile robots to investigate, each robot outfitted with a complex set of sensors, recorders and manipulators.  Each robot is radio-controlled and has a small nuclear reactor to power what sometimes ends up being an extended stay at the sites.

Recently a complex underground maze of the Desreverezam Civilization has been discovered.  SCArED sent in two robots to investigate and over the course of several months they have mapped out the entire maze.  But then disaster struck~---~due to some unforeseen anomaly they no longer have radio contact with the two robots (the researchers are torn between the cause of this: either some magnetoferritin crystals in the walls or angry poltergeists).  After several weeks of trying to regain contact with the two robots they finally have managed to open up a shared channel with them.  There's not much bandwidth in the connection, so they can only send three basic commands to the robots: (Move) Forward, (Turn) Left and (Turn) Right.  Since this is a shared channel they cannot send differentiated commands to each robot, so if they send the Forward command each robot will try to move forward simultaneously.  If there is an opening in front of them that's fine but if there's a wall in front of one of the robots it will bump into the wall and remain where it is.   Any Turn command will likewise cause the robots to turn in lockstep.

\begin{figure}[!h]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{maze}
\caption{Sample Input $1$.}
\label{fig:maze}
\end{figure}

The researchers would like to find the minimum number of Forward commands to get both robots out of the maze (the Turn commands take so little time and power that they can be ignored).  As soon as either is out of the maze they can return to the surface and ignore any future commands.  Consider the situation in Figure \ref{fig:maze} where both robots are currently facing South. One approach to get the robots out of the maze is to first get robot B out as quickly as possible by moving it to locations $(6,2)$, $(6,1)$, $(5,1)$, $(4,1)$ and then out of the maze.  This takes $5$ Forward moves and in the process robot A moves to $(3,1)$ and stays there, bumping against walls $4$ times.  After robot B is out robot A can exit  the maze in $6$ more Forward moves, for a total of $11$ moves.  However, if robot A is moved out as quickly as possible first and then robot B is moved out, only $8$ Forward moves are needed.  For other mazes, the minimum number of Forward moves may not involve getting either robot out as quickly as possible (see Sample Input~2).

Given a maze and the initial positions and orientations of the robots, help the researchers out by determining the minimum number of Forward commands to get both robots out of the maze.  If there are several sets of minimum Forward commands then the one that leads to the least number of wall bumps is preferred.  Oh, and one more thing: the robots should never be on top of each other inside the maze~---~this may lead to a chain reaction of their nuclear power generators leading to an explosion that could destroy the planet.  This should be avoided.

\section*{Input}
Input starts with a line containing $3$ positive integers $c$ $r$ $e$ $(c,r \leq 50, e \leq c)$  indicating the maze has $c$ columns and $r$ rows (all numbered starting at $1$) and that the exit to the maze is on the southern side of square $(e,1)$.  Following this is a line of the form $c_1$ $r_1$ $d_1$ $c_2$ $r_2$ $d_2$ ($1 \leq c_i \leq c, 1 \leq r_i \leq r, d_i \in \{\mbox{\tt N,E,S,W}\})$ indicating that the two robots are at locations $(c_1,r_1)$ and $(c_2,r_2)$ with orientations $d_1$ and $d_2$, respectively.  Locations $(c_1, r_1)$ and $(c_2, r_2)$ will never be the same, and $d_1$ and $d_2$ may be different.

After this come two lines which describe the maze.  The first line has the form $n$ $c_1$ $r_1$ $c_2$ $r_2 \ldots c_n$ $r_n$ ($n \leq r*c, 1 \leq c_i \leq c, 1 \leq r_i < r$) where each $c_i$ $r_i$ pair indicates that there is a horizontal wall between grid squares $(c_i,r_i)$ and $(c_i,r_i+1)$.  The next line is similar, where each $c_i$ $r_i$ pair ($n \leq r*c, 1 \leq c_i < c, 1 \leq r_i \leq r$) indicates that there is a vertical wall between grid squares $(c_i,r_i)$ and $(c_i+1,r_i)$.  All exterior walls except the exit are assumed to exist and are not listed in these two lines.

\section*{Output}
Output two values: the minimum number of Forward commands to get the two robots out of the maze and the number of bumps that occur using these commands.  If there are several ways to get the robots out with the same minimum number of Forward command, use the one that minimizes the number of bumps.   It will be possible for both robots to escape the maze in each input instance.

%\section*{Comment}
%The solution I envision is a breadth-first search of a graph with each node representing the joint locations of the robots.  With an n x m matrix there are only nm choose 2 possible nodes so that should guide us to the maximum size maze we can use.  The reason I only want to count the Move Forward command is that otherwise we also have to take into account the orientation of the robots which multiplies the number of nodes by 4.

%Question: Should we include the fact that some optimal solutions don't involve the optimal way to get any robot out individually (i.e., the solution to Sample Input 2) or should we let the contestants figure that out themselves.

Problem:
\problemname{Double Up}

A Double Up game consists of a sequence of $n$ numbers $a_1, \ldots, a_n$,
where each $a_i$ is a power of two. In one move one can either remove one of the numbers,
or merge two identical adjacent numbers into a single number of twice the value.
For example, for sequence $4,2,2,1,8$, we can merge the $2$s and obtain $4,4,1,8$,
then merge the $4$s and obtain $8,1,8$, then remove the $1$, and, finally,
merge the $8$s, obtaining a single final number, $16$. We play the game until a single number remains.
What is the largest number we can obtain?

\section*{Input}
The input consists of two lines. The first line contains $n$ ($1 \leq n \leq 1000$).
The second line contains numbers $a_1, \ldots, a_n$, where $1\leq a_i\leq 2^{100}$ for each $i$.

\section*{Output}
The ouput consists of a single line containing the largest number that can be obtained from the input sequence $a_1, \ldots, a_n$.




Sample Input 1:
5
4 2 2 1 8

Sample Output 1:
16



Your complete Python 3 program:
response_text:
```python
def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    # We can think of this problem recursively or with a greedy approach.
    # The key insight is that we want to merge as many numbers as possible,
    # and we should merge from the right to accumulate the maximum value.
    
    # A better approach: we can use a stack-like method.
    # Process numbers from left to right, and try to merge whenever possible.
    
    stack = []
    for num in a:
        # Try to merge with previous numbers
        while stack and stack[-1] == num:
            # Merge the two identical numbers
            num = num * 2
            stack.pop()
        stack.append(num)
    
    # The maximum number will be the maximum in the stack
    # But actually, we want to find the best way to get one number.
    # Let's reconsider the approach.
    
    # The optimal strategy is to work backwards and try to build the largest possible number.
    # But a simpler and correct approach:
    # Process from left to right with a stack. When we see a number that can merge
    # with the top of the stack, we merge it. This is greedy and optimal.
    
    stack = []
    for num in a:
        while stack and stack[-1] == num:
            num = num * 2
            stack.pop()
        stack.append(num)
    
    # Find the maximum value in the final stack
    result = max(stack)
    print(result)

solve()
```

The output should be the string `up` if the blueberry side is facing up after the pan returns to horizontal via minimal rotation, or `down` otherwise.

INVARIANTS:  
- The total rotation in degrees is $ \theta = \frac{180f}{r} $.  
- The final orientation before returning to horizontal is determined modulo 360 degrees.  
- The pan will rotate back minimally (less than 90 degrees) to reach a horizontal position.  
- The blueberry side is initially up; flipping occurs every 180 degrees of net rotation.

3. Determine whether minimal correction brings blueberry side up or down.

4. Apply minimal correction and decide final orientation.

2. If $ f \bmod r \ne 0 $, there's a partial rotation.

2. For each subarray, compute the best value that can be obtained by merging or removing elements.
3. Use interval merging: for a segment, try all possible splits and compute the best value.
4. Memoize results to avoid recomputation.
5. The base case is a single element.
6. The transition considers merging two equal adjacent elements or removing the smallest element.

\problemname{A-Mazing Puzzle}

The Severely Challenging Archaeological Exploration Division (SCArED) specializes in the investigation of archaeological sites that are too difficult, dangerous or downright terrifying for humans to examine firsthand.  Instead they send in one or more mobile robots to investigate, each robot outfitted with a complex set of sensors, recorders and manipulators.  Each robot is radio-controlled and has a small nuclear reactor to power what sometimes ends up being an extended stay at the sites.

Recently a complex underground maze of the Desreverezam Civilization has been discovered.  SCArED sent in two robots to investigate and over the course of several months they have mapped out the entire maze.  But then disaster struck~---~due to some unforeseen anomaly they no longer have radio contact with the two robots (the researchers are torn between the cause of this: either some magnetoferritin crystals in the walls or angry poltergeists).  After several weeks of trying to regain contact with the two robots they finally have managed to open up a shared channel with them.  There's not much bandwidth in the connection, so they can only send three basic commands to the robots: (Move) Forward, (Turn) Left and (Turn) Right.  Since this is a shared channel they cannot send differentiated commands to each robot, so if they send the Forward command each robot will try to move forward simultaneously.  If there is an opening in front of them that's fine but if there's a wall in front of one of the robots it will bump into the wall and remain where it is.   Any Turn command will likewise cause the robots to turn in lockstep.

\begin{figure}[!h]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{maze}
\caption{Sample Input $1$.}
\label{fig:maze}
\end{figure}

The researchers would like to find the minimum number of Forward commands to get both robots out of the maze (the Turn commands take so little time and power that they can be ignored).  As soon as either is out of the maze they can return to the surface and ignore any future commands.  Consider the situation in Figure \ref{fig:maze} where both robots are currently facing South. One approach to get the robots out of the maze is to first get robot B out as quickly as possible by moving it to locations $(6,2)$, $(6,1)$, $(5,1)$, $(4,1)$ and then out of the maze.  This takes $5$ Forward moves and in the process robot A moves to $(3,1)$ and stays there, bumping against walls $4$ times.  After robot B is out robot A can exit  the maze in $6$ more Forward moves, for a total of $11$ moves.  However, if robot A is moved out as quickly as possible first and then robot B is moved out, only $8$ Forward moves are needed.  For other mazes, the minimum number of Forward moves may not involve getting either robot out as quickly as possible (see Sample Input~2).

Given a maze and the initial positions and orientations of the robots, help the researchers out by determining the minimum number of Forward commands to get both robots out of the maze.  If there are several sets of minimum Forward commands then the one that leads to the least number of wall bumps is preferred.  Oh, and one more thing: the robots should never be on top of each other inside the maze~---~this may lead to a chain reaction of their nuclear power generators leading to an explosion that could destroy the planet.  This should be avoided.

\section*{Input}
Input starts with a line containing $3$ positive integers $c$ $r$ $e$ $(c,r \leq 50, e \leq c)$  indicating the maze has $c$ columns and $r$ rows (all numbered starting at $1$) and that the exit to the maze is on the southern side of square $(e,1)$.  Following this is a line of the form $c_1$ $r_1$ $d_1$ $c_2$ $r_2$ $d_2$ ($1 \leq c_i \leq c, 1 \leq r_i \leq r, d_i \in \{\mbox{\tt N,E,S,W}\})$ indicating that the two robots are at locations $(c_1,r_1)$ and $(c_2,r_2)$ with orientations $d_1$ and $d_2$, respectively.  Locations $(c_1, r_1)$ and $(c_2, r_2)$ will never be the same, and $d_1$ and $d_2$ may be different.

After this come two lines which describe the maze.  The first line has the form $n$ $c_1$ $r_1$ $c_2$ $r_2 \ldots c_n$ $r_n$ ($n \leq r*c, 1 \leq c_i \leq c, 1 \leq r_i < r$) where each $c_i$ $r_i$ pair indicates that there is a horizontal wall between grid squares $(c_i,r_i)$ and $(c_i,r_i+1)$. All exterior walls except the exit are assumed to exist and are not listed in these two lines.

Output two values: the minimum number of Forward commands to get the two robots out of the maze and the number of bumps that occur using these commands.  If there are several ways to get the robots out with the same minimum number of Forward command, use the one that minimizes the number of bumps.   It will be possible for both robots to escape the maze in each input instance.

r, f = map(int, input().split())

# Calculate total rotation in degrees
# 180 degrees every r seconds, so 180/r degrees per second
# After f seconds: (180/r) * f degrees
total_rotation = (180 * f) / r

# Normalize the rotation to [0, 360) range
total_rotation = total_rotation % 360

# After stopping, the pan returns to horizontal with minimal rotation (< 90 degrees)
# So we need to see what the final position would be:
# If rotation is in [0, 90), it rotates back to 0 (horizontal)
# If rotation is in [90, 270), it rotates to 180 (upside down) 
# If rotation is in [270, 360), it rotates back to 0 (horizontal)

# But we need to be more precise:
# The pan stops at some angle, then rotates minimally to become horizontal
# If the angle is in [0, 90], rotate backward to 0
# If the angle is in (90, 270), rotate forward to 180
# If the angle is in [270, 360), rotate backward to 360 (which is same as 0)

# However, since we're told it's never vertical when it stops, we don't have exact 90 or 270.
# So:
# If total_rotation is in [0, 90), final position is 0 (horizontal, blueberry up)
# If total_rotation is in (90, 270), final position is 180 (upside down, blueberry down)
# If total_rotation is in (270, 360), final position is 0 (horizontal, blueberry up)

# But wait, let's think about this differently.
# The waffle starts blueberry side up (at 0 degrees).
# As the pan rotates, we track the orientation.
# When it stops rotating, it adjusts to horizontal with minimal rotation.

# Let's think of it this way:
# - 0 degrees: blueberry up
# - 180 degrees: blueberry down
# So we just need to know the final orientation mod 360, then see if it's closer to 0 or 180

# Actually, let's reframe:
# The pan rotates total_rotation degrees.
# Then it rotates minimally to become horizontal.
# So if it stopped at angle α, it will rotate by -α to become horizontal (if α ∈ [0,90] ∪ (270,360))
# or by (180-α) to become horizontal at 180 degrees (if α ∈ (90, 270))

# But there's an easier way:
# Just figure out where it ends up after the minimal rotation to horizontal.

if (total_rotation < 90) or (total_rotation > 270):
    # Final position is horizontal (0 degrees) - blueberry up
    print("up")
else:
    # Final position is upside down (180 degrees) - blueberry down
    print("down")
```

from collections import deque
import sys

def read_input():
    import sys
    input = sys.stdin.read
    data = input().split()
    idx = 0
    n = int(data[idx])
    idx += 1
    tubes = []
    for _ in range(n + 1):
        b = int(data[idx])
        m = int(data[idx+1])
        t = int(data[idx+2])
        idx += 3
        # Represent tube as list from bottom to top, ignoring zeros at the end
        tube = [b, m, t]
        # Remove trailing zeros
        while tube and tube[-1] == 0:
            tube.pop()
        tubes.append(tube)
    return n, tubes

def is_solved(tubes):
    for tube in tubes:
        if len(tube) == 0:
            continue
        if len(tube) != 3:
            return False
        if not (tube[0] == tube[1] == tube[2] and tube[0] != 0):
            return False
    return True

def tubes_to_tuple(tubes):
    # Convert list of lists to tuple of tuples for hashing
    return tuple(tuple(tube) for tube in tubes)

def tuple_to_tubes(tup):
    # Convert back to list of lists
    return [list(tube) for tube in tup]

def get_next_states(tubes):
    next_states = []
    moves = []
    n_tubes = len(tubes)
    for i in range(n_tubes):
        if not tubes[i]:  # Empty tube, nothing to move
            continue
        # Get the top ball
        top_ball = tubes[i][-1]
        for j in range(n_tubes):
            if i == j:
                continue
            if len(tubes[j]) < 3:  # There's space
                # Make a copy of tubes
                new_tubes = [tube[:] for tube in tubes]
                # Pop from i
                ball = new_tubes[i].pop()
                # Push to j
                new_tubes[j].append(ball)
                next_states.append(new_tubes)
                moves.append((i+1, j+1))  # 1-indexed
    return next_states, moves

def bfs_solve(n, initial_tubes):
    if is_solved(initial_tubes):
        return 0, []
    queue = deque()
    queue.append((initial_tubes, []))  # state, move_list
    visited = set()
    visited.add(tubes_to_tuple(initial_tubes))
    max_moves = 20 * n

Problem:
\problemname{Sun and Moon}

You recently missed an eclipse and are waiting for the next one!  To
see any eclipse from your home, the sun and the moon must be in
alignment at specific positions.  You know how many years ago the sun was in the right position, and how many years it takes for it to get back to that position.
You know the same for the moon.
When will you see the next eclipse?

\section*{Input}

The input consists of two lines.

The first line contains two integers, $d_s$ and $y_s$
$(0 \le d_s < y_s \le 50)$, where $d_s$ is how many years ago the sun was in the right position, and $y_s$ is how many years it takes for the sun to be back in that position.

The second line contains two integers, $d_m$ and $y_m$
$(0 \le d_m < y_m \le 50)$, where $d_m$ is how many years ago the moon was in the right position, and $y_m$ is how many years it takes for the moon to be back in that position. 


\section*{Output}

Output a single integer, the number of years until the next
eclipse. The data will be set in such a way that there is not an eclipse happening right now
and there will be an eclipse within the next $5\,000$ years.

3 10
1 2

Sample Output 1:
7

response_text:

d_s, y_s = map(int, input().split())
d_m, y_m = map(int, input().split())

# Sun was at the right position d_s years ago, so it will be at the right position again
# in (y_s - d_s) years, then every y_s years after that.
# So the sun will be at the right position at years: (y_s - d_s), (y_s - d_s) + y_s, (y_s - d_s) + 2*y_s, ...

# Similarly for the moon: it will be at the right position in (y_m - d_m) years,
# then every y_m years after that.

# We want to find the first time both the sun and moon are at the right position at the same time.
# This is equivalent to finding the smallest positive t such that:
#   t ≡ (y_s - d_s) (mod y_s)
#   t ≡ (y_m - d_m) (mod y_m)

# This is a system of two modular equations. We can solve it by checking all multiples
# of one of them and see if they satisfy the other.

sun_start = (y_s - d_s) % y_s
moon_start = (y_m - d_m) % y_m

# We'll iterate through the years when the sun is in the right position
# and check if the moon is also in the right position.

t = sun_start
while t % y_m != moon_start:
    t += y_s

print(t)
```
